summary:
The Treaty of Brest-Litovsk, signed on March 3, 1918, ended Russian participation in [[event:110|World War I]] following the [[event:2646|Bolshevik revolution]]. Under the treaty's harsh terms, Russia ceded approximately one third of its European territory to Germany and its allies, including the Baltic states, Finland, Poland, Ukraine, and Transcaucasia. The territories lost contained about a third of Russia's population, half of its industrial facilities, and the majority of its coal mines. The Bolsheviks, facing military collapse and needing peace to consolidate their revolution against multiple internal enemies, accepted these terms despite fierce debate within the party.

Leon Trotsky, the lead Soviet negotiator, initially tried a strategy of 'neither war nor peace' — refusing to sign but also refusing to continue fighting — but German forces resumed their advance, forcing the Bolsheviks to accept even harsher terms than originally proposed. Lenin argued that accepting any peace was necessary to preserve the revolution, even a humiliating one, expecting that socialist revolution in Germany would soon render the treaty moot.

The treaty was annulled in November 1918 following Germany's defeat on the Western Front and the German Revolution. Russia recovered most of the surrendered territories, though Poland, Finland, and the Baltic states established independent states. The treaty nonetheless demonstrated the fragility of the Russian position and reinforced the Bolsheviks' determination to build military strength, contributing to the eventual creation of the Red Army.
